The seventeen-witness DIMEV census is retained as catalog evidence rather than flattened into Horstmann's direct lineage.
Manuscript date, production place, and dialect are kept distinct from work composition, and scan-file rights are kept distinct from public-domain textual content.

lineage.summary: CME00107 is the repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of Carl Horstmann's 1874 edition of the Middle English Life of Saint Brendan. Horstmann printed an Ashmole 43 base text and supplied variant apparatus from Harley 2277, Laud Misc. 108, and Bodley 779. Complete Digital Bodleian image sequences are locally cached for the three Oxford witnesses and mapped to the exact work folios; the British Library currently exposes a catalog record and legacy viewer link for Harley 2277, but its images were unavailable when checked. Horstmann's route to the Harley readings is modeled through Thomas Wright's 1844 printed transcription because that is the intermediary Horstmann explicitly cites; direct manuscript consultation remains unresolved.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English author or adapter
catalogSummary.date: Composed 1250-1290, with circa 1260 suggested; extant by the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century; Horstmann edition published 1874
catalogSummary.region: England; the original dialect is only broadly southern and no precise place of composition is established
catalogSummary.title: Life of Saint Brendan

catalogingSubject.label: Life of Saint Brendan
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is the anonymous Middle English verse Life of Saint Brendan transmitted as part of the South English Legendary. CME00107 represents Carl Horstmann's 1874 witness-based edition: a running text from Bodleian MS Ashmole 43, ff. 71v-80v, with variant material associated with British Library Harley MS 2277, Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 108, and Bodleian MS Bodley 779. Horstmann's German headnote and textual apparatus are modeled as an editorial content part, not as a second medieval work. The record does not describe the whole South English Legendary, every one of the seventeen DIMEV witnesses, Thomas Wright's prose Brendan, or the physical codices as if they were the work itself.

contentParts.label: Life of Saint Brendan
notes: The 733 count is the main XML stream. It is not silently reconciled with DIMEV's 738, Lavery's 734, or PCMEP's 732 plus final Amen.
Harley, Laud, and Bodley readings are source apparatus for this work, not additional content works.
contentParts.summary: The anonymous Middle English verse saints' life, represented by Horstmann's Ashmole 43 running text.

contentParts.label: Horstmann's headnote and textual apparatus

locators.value: DIV1 direct P plus NOTE elements and three DIV2 variant divisions
ff. 41v-51r, represented through Wright's printed metrical text unless direct Horstmann consultation is established

notes: The three item count refers to the three named variant divisions, not the total number of individual variant readings.
The printed article interleaves Harley variants with the running poem; the XML serializes them after the main verse group.
contentParts.summary: German editorial framing, embedded notes, and witness-specific readings associated with Harley 2277, Laud Misc. 108, and Bodley 779.

assertion.notes: A specialist composition range for the abstract poem; it is not inferred from the date of any one surviving witness.
The codex's broader 1290-1400 history includes later additions; the target belongs to its early core.

dateStatements.display: Extant by the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century in Laud Misc. 108

scope.description: Current earliest attestation among the immediate witnesses researched
Publication of the immediate source edition, not the first circulation of the medieval work

extent.description: The cataloged unit contains one medieval verse work plus one editorial-apparatus part. The XML has one DIV1, three DIV2 elements, four HEAD elements, two LG elements, 737 total L elements, 733 main-work L elements, four Harley-apparatus L elements, 156 NOTE elements, 53 PB elements, 164 MILESTONE elements, 28 P elements, and 1,081 HI elements. Total XML lines include apparatus and therefore are not a work-level line count. DIMEV reports 738, Lavery 734, and PCMEP 733 as 732 plus Amen; those measures remain explicitly unresolved.

formStatements.description: A Middle English poem in rhyming aa couplets, commonly described as septenary with seven stresses and two half-lines.

scope.description: Work-level form; the separately modeled editorial apparatus does not make the medieval work mixed

formStatements.description: German prose framing and variant notation with four Harley readings encoded as verse lines.

assertion.notes: A descriptive narrative mode within the saints' life; not used here to reclassify the poem as secular romance.
Lavery emphasizes the plain vernacular didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary adaptation.

notes: The preferred title is a modern supplied work title; witness rubrics and Horstmann's German article title remain separately identified.
Saint Brendan is the subject, not an attributed author. The medieval author or adapter is anonymous.
German belongs to Horstmann's retained editorial framing and remains described in the content boundary; it is not exposed as an intrinsic language or genre of the medieval work.
The main work is verse. The record's mixed editorial-apparatus part does not change the work-level form to mixed.
Romance is not assigned as a primary genre: the voyage contains adventure and marvels, but the strongest grounded classification is hagiographic saints' legend and religious poetry.

All three Oxford facsimiles are complete manuscript sequences locally cached and precisely mapped to work folios; their image rights are CC BY-NC 4.0 and do not authorize for-profit facsimile redistribution.
The record remains partial because the line-count conventions, Latin source recension, original linguistic localization, and exact Harley collation route remain unresolved.

next_steps: Collate lineation in Horstmann, Wright, the four manuscripts, and a current critical edition.
Record separately whether Amen, rubrics, split lines, lacunae, and witness-specific additions are counted.
Retain 733 only as the present main-stream XML count until reconciled.
openQuestions.question: How should the 733 main XML lines, DIMEV's 738 lines, Lavery's 734 lines, and PCMEP's 732 lines plus final Amen be reconciled across witnesses and editorial conventions?

next_steps: Consult scholarship published after Lavery's 1984 study.
Compare distinctive narrative and verbal readings against the Latin recension tradition.
Keep probable work-level adaptation separate from any claim about a physical exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Which Latin Navigatio Sancti Brendani recension or exemplar most directly underlies the South English Legendary adaptation?

next_steps: Consult current linguistic and South English Legendary scholarship on reconstructed archetypal language.
Separate original-language claims from the Gloucestershire, Somerset, and Hampshire profiles of individual witnesses.
Retain England as the broad cultural origin and southern England as tentative only.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's place and dialect of composition be localized more closely than broadly southern England without transferring properties from later witnesses?

next_steps: Compare Horstmann's Harley apparatus against Wright once manuscript images are again accessible.
Search Horstmann's methodological statements, correspondence, and working papers.
Retain the Wright intermediary and do not state direct consultation until evidenced.
openQuestions.question: Did Horstmann consult Harley MS 2277 directly, or is every Harley reading in CME00107 mediated by Wright's 1844 transcription?

assertion.notes: The work is Middle English and part of the South English Legendary tradition; a more precise place of composition is not established.
PCMEP reports that the original dialect is unknown, though northern origin can be ruled out; Gloucestershire, Somerset, and Hampshire assignments belong to particular witnesses.

The 1874 edited representation, including base text and apparatus

assertion.notes: The Harley route is qualified in the lineage record: Wright's 1844 printed transcription is the explicit supported intermediary, and direct consultation of Harley by Horstmann remains unresolved.

scope.description: Readings associated with Harley 2277, Laud Misc. 108, and Bodley 779

subjects.label: Terrestrial Paradise and the Land of Promise

summaries.value: A Middle English verse life recounting Brendan's sacred sea voyage with a company of monks. The voyagers pass providential and marvelous islands, encounter the whale Jasconius, birds associated with fallen angels, demons and Judas, hermits, and paradisal spaces, and ultimately reach the Land of Promise before Brendan returns and dies. The narrative adapts hagiographic voyage material to the concise didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary.

CME00107 contains a 733-line main poem encoded in one principal verse group, followed by serialized editorial apparatus: a German headnote, 156 notes in the complete file, four verse lines embedded in the Harley variants, and separate Harley, Laud, and Bodley variant divisions. The printed source spans pp. 17-48.

The work is represented through Horstmann's Ashmole-based edition. Ashmole 43 is the base witness; Laud Misc. 108 and Bodley 779 are directly labeled variant witnesses; Harley 2277 reaches the securely supported lineage through Wright's 1844 printed transcription because Horstmann explicitly cites Wright, while Horstmann's own direct consultation of Harley remains unproved. DIMEV lists thirteen additional witnesses that are relevant to the wider textual tradition but are not immediate sources of CME00107.

Work, witness, edition, and encoding layers are kept separate. The thirteenth-century composition range belongs to the abstract poem; dates, regions, and dialects assigned to individual codices describe those witnesses; 1874 describes Horstmann's publication; and XML structural counts describe the digital representation. The work-level record therefore does not inherit Gloucestershire, Somerset, Hampshire, or Oxford as a precise place of composition.

The voyagers pass providential and marvelous islands, encounter the whale Jasconius, birds associated with fallen angels, demons and Judas, hermits, and paradisal spaces, and ultimately reach the Land of Promise before Brendan returns and dies. The narrative adapts hagiographic voyage material to the concise didactic and homiletic manner of the South English Legendary.
